name: Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop
name_original: Sendero Medieval de La Alpujarra – trail running circular entre Busquístar y Cástaras (PR-A 299)
description: Jog the difficult 9.9-mile Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop, gaining 2844 feet in 2h 25m through historic villages and mountain views.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Busquístar are situated on the southern slopes of the Sierra Nevada Natural Park, at an elevation of 1100 meters above sea level. The region features varied terrain, including extensive chestnut forests and the dramatic Trevélez River Valley. This landscape offers a mix of ascents and descents, providing diverse conditions for running. The area is characterized by its mountain environment and network of trails. Best jogging routes around Busquístar The most popular jogging route is Peak Peñabón – trail running circular from Trevélez (SL-A 78), a 5.7 miles (9.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 28 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local runners is Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop, a difficult 9.9 miles (15.9 km) path. This route traverses historical paths with substantial elevation changes. Local runners also love the Medieval Alpujarra Trail, a 4.3 miles (7.0 km) trail leading through the Alpujarra region, often completed in about 57 minutes. Jogging around Busquístar is defined by mountain trails, chestnut forests, and river valleys. The extensive network offers options for both moderate and difficult running abilities. The routes in Busquístar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 4 reviews. More than 300 runners have used komoot to explore Busquístar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Busquístar?
A: Busquístar offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 35 trails available for exploration. These routes vary in difficulty, catering to different fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails in Busquístar?
A: The running trails in Busquístar are characterized by their varied and often jagged terrain. You'll encounter a mix of ascents and descents, unpaved paths, and hiking trails. Many routes pass through lush chestnut forests and offer impressive views of the Trevélez River Valley, providing a diverse and invigorating experience.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy jog?
A: While Busquístar is known for its challenging mountain trails, there are some moderate options available. For example, sections of the GR-7 path offer less strenuous stretches. It's always recommended to check the route details for elevation and distance to find a path that matches your fitness level.

Q: What makes the running routes in Busquístar challenging?
A: Many running routes in Busquístar are considered difficult due to significant elevation changes and the rugged, mountainous terrain of the Sierra Nevada Natural Park. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986546426/medieval-trail-of-the-alpujarra-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 9.9-mile path with substantial elevation changes, ideal for experienced trail runners.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints or natural landmarks to see while running in Busquístar?
A: Absolutely! The region is rich in natural beauty. You can find routes with panoramic views of the Trevélez River Gorge, and some trails lead to impressive spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288705" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo de Cortés Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. The area also features stunning mountain peaks, including views towards &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñabón&lt;/a&gt; and even distant glimpses of Mulhacén.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through the famous chestnut forests?
A: Yes, Busquístar is surrounded by extensive and beautiful chestnut forests. Many trails weave through these shaded woodlands, offering a refreshing environment and particularly stunning scenery during autumn. These forests are a highlight of running in the region.

Q: Are there any waterfalls along the running trails near Busquístar?
A: Yes, you can encounter beautiful waterfalls on some routes. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288705" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo de Cortés Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which is a popular highlight in the gorges around Busquístar and can be incorporated into longer runs.

Q: What historical or cultural sites can I explore on a run in Busquístar?
A: The area around Busquístar is steeped in history. Many running paths follow ancient routes, including sections of the long-distance GR-7 path and the Medieval Path of La Alpujarra. These trails connect traditional Alpujarran villages like Pórtugos, Atalbéitar, and Ferreirola, allowing you to experience the unique architecture and cultural heritage while you run.

Q: Are there circular running routes available in Busquístar?
A: Yes, Busquístar offers several excellent circular running routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986619461/peak-penabon-trail-running-circular-from-trevelez-sl-a-78" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peak Peñabón – trail running circular from Trevélez (SL-A 78)&lt;/a&gt;, a 5.7-mile trail with significant elevation gain. Another great choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38258748" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Busquístar – Mecina Fondales loop from Busquístar&lt;/a&gt;, which is a longer, challenging loop.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Busquístar?
A: The clean mountain air makes Busquístar enjoyable for running for much of the year. Spring and autumn are particularly pleasant, with milder temperatures and beautiful natural colors, especially when the chestnut forests change in fall. Summer mornings are also great, but it's advisable to avoid the midday heat. Winters can be cold at 1100 meters above sea level, with potential for snow at higher elevations.

Q: Are the running trails in Busquístar dog-friendly?
A: Many trails in the Sierra Nevada Natural Park are generally accessible for dogs, provided they are kept under control, especially in areas with livestock or wildlife. It's always best to check specific trail regulations and ensure your dog is fit for the varied terrain and elevation changes. Remember to carry water for both yourself and your pet.

Q: Are there family-friendly running paths in Busquístar?
A: While many routes are challenging, some sections of the extensive trail network can be suitable for families looking for a less strenuous jog or walk. Look for paths with gentler gradients and shorter distances. Exploring the historical paths between villages can also be a rewarding family activity, allowing for breaks and cultural exploration.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Busquístar?
A: The running routes in Busquístar are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 4 reviews. Runners often praise the stunning mountain environment, the diverse terrain, and the extensive network of trails that offer options for both moderate and difficult running abilities.
tips: This is one of the mightiest rivers in Granada thanks to the melting of the snow from the Sierra Nevada. Some people also consider it "the highest river in Spain" although, of course, this depends on what is considered a river and it is not easy information to verify.
Notáez is a key place to learn about the history of the last Moors, their resistance, persecution and subsequent imprisonment in the Alhambra in Granada or slavery.

Busquístar is part of the Sierra Nevada Natural Park, which extends the protection of the National Park by almost 89,000 more hectares. The town is surrounded by chestnut forests and crossed by the ever mighty Trevélez River.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog the Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for jogging this 15.9 km loop is around 2 hours and 25 minutes, based on average speeds. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this jogging route?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult for jogging. It features significant elevation changes, with over 860 meters of ascent and descent, and varied terrain that requires good fitness and sure-footedness.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain should I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: Expect diverse terrain, including ancient medieval paths that can be stony, rocky, or even muddy after rain. You'll encounter both paved and unpaved surfaces, including cobbled paths known as 'carihuelas'. Steep ascents and descents are common, especially when traversing valleys and gorges, with some sections like the climb from the Trevélez River being particularly tough.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and natural features along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking landscapes, passing through picturesque white villages with Moorish architecture, such as Busquístar and Notáez Village. You'll see steep gorges, chestnut woods, ancient irrigation channels (acequias), and panoramic views of the Sierra Nevada. The Trevélez River is another notable highlight.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: Spring and early summer are ideal, as the flora is vibrant with wildflowers. Clear days can offer views down to the Mediterranean Sea. Be prepared for potentially muddy or overgrown sections, especially after winter.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails in the Alpujarra?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails, including the Ruta Medieval de la Alpujarra (PR-A 299), [CIMA GR14] Trevélez, Andalucía - South Route (Provincia de Granada), Transandalus, and Transnevada (Stage 6 / 8).

faqs.question: Where can I park for the Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop?
faqs.answer: The loop starts and ends between Busquístar and Cástaras. You should look for parking options in or near these villages to access the trailhead conveniently. Specific parking areas are usually signposted within the villages.

faqs.question: Is the Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail passes through natural areas and villages, it's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash due to varied terrain, potential livestock, and local regulations. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to jog the Medieval Trail of the Alpujarra Loop. It is a public access route.

faqs.question: What should I wear or bring for jogging the trail, especially in summer?
faqs.answer: Given the difficult rating and varied terrain, sturdy trail running shoes with good grip are essential. In summer, wear lightweight, breathable clothing, a hat, and sunglasses. Always carry sufficient water, as natural springs may not always be reliable, and sun protection is crucial. A map or GPS device is also recommended.

faqs.question: Are there places to get water or find facilities along the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail passes through several white villages like Busquístar and Notáez Village where you may find cafes or small shops for refreshments. You might also encounter natural springs, but it's always best to carry your own water supply.
